coredata.dc:title: Enhanced uranium uptake from acidic media achieved on a novel iron phosphate adsorbent
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Ning
Tang, Jiao
Hou, Dezhi
Lei, Hao
Zhou, Daohui
Ding, Jie
coredata.dc:description: By combining phosphoric acid precursor synthesis and co-precipitation method, we developed a facile approach for synthesizing iron phosphate (FeyPz) with repeatable technique and low economic cost. The procedure begins with synthesis of P-doped carbon dot (pCD), followed by introducing the pCD in the preparation condition of Fe3O4. Interesting, the uranium(U(VI)) adsorption capacity of FeyPz is significantly influenced by the synthesis conditions of FeyPz and the optimized performance can be achieved by tuning the functional groups and structure of FeyPz. Compared to the FePO4٠2H2O and Fe3O4, the FeyPz-10 showed 3561% and 684.5% enhanced performance in U(VI) uptake, respectively. The maximum adsorption capacity of FeyPz-10 based on Langmuir model could reach up to 1.22 mmol g−1 at pH 4.5, which was comparable with the saturated adsorption capacity based on Thomas model with its value of 0.87 mmol g−1, that was higher than some of adsorbents reported previously. In addition, the adsorbent had a superior reusability and still kept up high adsorption capacity for U(VI) after 5th cycle. Moreover, an adsorption-reduction mechanism was proposed and XPS analysis confirmed the outstanding adsorption capacity for U(VI) on the adsorbent was mainly ascribed to the transformation of Fe2+ to Fe3+ on FeyPz-10 that results in U(VI) reduction.

head.ce:title: Enhanced uranium uptake from acidic media achieved on a novel iron phosphate adsorbent

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: By combining phosphoric acid precursor synthesis and co-precipitation method, we developed a facile approach for synthesizing iron phosphate (FeyPz) with repeatable technique and low economic cost. The procedure begins with synthesis of P-doped carbon dot (pCD), followed by introducing the pCD in the preparation condition of Fe3O4. Interesting, the uranium(U(VI)) adsorption capacity of FeyPz is significantly influenced by the synthesis conditions of FeyPz and the optimized performance can be achieved by tuning the functional groups and structure of FeyPz. Compared to the FePO4٠2H2O and Fe3O4, the FeyPz-10 showed 3561% and 684.5% enhanced performance in U(VI) uptake, respectively. The maximum adsorption capacity of FeyPz-10 based on Langmuir model could reach up to 1.22 mmol g−1 at pH 4.5, which was comparable with the saturated adsorption capacity based on Thomas model with its value of 0.87 mmol g−1, that was higher than some of adsorbents reported previously. In addition, the adsorbent had a superior reusability and still kept up high adsorption capacity for U(VI) after 5th cycle. Moreover, an adsorption-reduction mechanism was proposed and XPS analysis confirmed the outstanding adsorption capacity for U(VI) on the adsorbent was mainly ascribed to the transformation of Fe2+ to Fe3+ on FeyPz-10 that results in U(VI) reduction.

ce:section.ce:para: Attaining a balance between nuclear safety and the usage of uranium (U) resource and the ever-increasing need for raw materials required to generate nuclear electricity is the requirement of sustainable development of nuclear energy. Attributing to hydrometallurgical processes of uranium ore, scientific research, spent nuclear fuel reprocessing (approximately 95% of spent nuclear fuel is uranium) and the disposal of nuclear wastes, an appreciable quantity of uranium found in various wastewater process streams which mainly in the form of uranyl ions (UO2 2+) which exhibits high solubility, high mobility and biological toxicity that pose major threats to the ecological environment and humans [1–3]. Waste minimization and achieving effective approaches for effective remediation of uranyl ions in polluted water with cost-effective are critical to promote environmental safety and human health.

Currently, some ion adsorbents such as covalent organic frameworks [1–2], metal–organic frameworks [4], phosphorylated carbon aerogel [5], binary hydroxides [6], mixed metal oxides [7] and minerals [3,8–10] have been developed for this purpose. Nevertheless, the complicated synthesize route, costly chemicals, expensive equipment, or limited removal performance making it extremely challenging.

In particular, the most successful adsorbents based on phosphate functional groups which exhibit specific binding affinities towards U(VI) that has shown an extremely high U(VI) adsorption capacity in excess of 0.9 g g−1 (pH = 5.0) for phosphate functionalized layered double hydroxides [11].

Recently, there are increasing reports on for phosphonate material (zirconium phosphonate [12], hafnium phosphonate [13], but fewer on metal phosphate (titanium phosphates) [14], especially on iron (II, III) phosphate for U(VI) capture.

The zirconium phosphonate was shown to be ultrastable in fuming acids and was reported to exhibit high adsorption capacity for U(VI) (0.24 mmol g−1, pH = 4.5) [12]. The hafnium phosphonate was also found to exhibit high affinity for U(VI) with an distribution coefficient (K d) value of 5.09 × 103 mL g−1 in 3 mol L-1 HNO3 and high U(VI) uptake capacity of 0.27 mmol g−1 at pH = 1.0 [13]. The titanium phosphate was reported to exhibit an ultrahigh adsorption capacity (up to 5.25 mmol g−1 at pH = 2.0) based on the combination of ion exchange, surface complexation and precipitation reactions [14].

Recently, iron (III or II) phosphate is a promising alternative raw material for synthesis of LiFePO4 that have been widely demonstrated as a cathode material for application in lithium-ion battery because of its superior electrochemical and environmental friendliness properties [15]. Attributing to the high affinity between metal ions and phosphate and the special of oxidative/reductive capacity of iron (III/II) to metal ion with varied oxidation states, it has proven to be ideal for capturing metal ions such as lead complexes (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid-Pb) [16], arsenic (III, V) [17]. Inspired by the redox reactivity of Fe(II) to U(VI), the Fe(II)-containing material is an efficient adsorbent that have been demonstrated for capture of U(VI) from aqueous solution via a reduction mechanism [6,18–19].

Here, we reported not only a new iron (II/III) phosphate material (FeyPz-10) but also a highly efficient uranium adsorbent. First, we provide a two-step approach for the synthesis of FeyPz-10 by using P-doped carbon dot (pCD) as phosphoric acid source and the reaction of pCD and Fe3+/Fe2+ in a basic solution. Subsequently, the FeyPz-10 was characterized in detail using a variety of spectroscopic techniques. Later on, the U(VI) adsorption performance of FeyPz-10 was investigated by the batch adsorption experiments. The effect of parameters on the U(VI) adsorption were studied, including the pH value of solution, contact time, initial concentration of U(VI), and ionic strength. Importantly, the reusability of FeyPz-10 and adsorption column tests were also performed to inspect its potential for possibly actual application in the future. Lastly, the possible adsorption mechanism is explored.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA, C6H8O7·H2O, 99.5%), ethylenediamine (EA), H3PO4 (P, 85%), FeCl3·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, NaOH, ethanol, and the other chemical reagents were provided by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). And those reagents were of analytical pure and received without further purification.

Typically, pCD-6 was prepared by a hydrothermal method. Firstly, CA (1.5 g) and EA (0.5 mL) were added into a 50 mL beaker, and then deionized (DI) water (10 mL), H3PO4 (2 mL) were added under magnetic stirring conditions. After sonication for 0.5 h, the transparent solution was transferred to a 100 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined stainless-steel autoclave, sealed, and heated at 150 ℃ for 5 h in an oven. After cooling down to room temperature (RT) by naturally, the solution was poured into a 50 mL of crucible and dried to a constant weight at 100 ℃ for 48 h. Finally, the brown slurry was obtained. Besides, a series of pCD-x (x = 1–5) was also prepared by changing the amounts of EA and H3PO4 which were marked as factor-E and F, respectively (as listed in Table S1
-S2).

Fe3O4 was prepared by a simple co-precipitation method with Fe3+/Fe2+ mole ratio of 2.0. Briefly, FeCl3·6H2O (0.54 g, 0.002 mol) and FeSO4·7H2O (0.28 g, 0.001 mol) were dissolved in 10 mL of DI water. After vigorously stirred for 10 min, it was placed in an oil batch set to 90 ℃, followed by slowly dropping NaOH aqueous solution (0.4 g of NaOH /10 mL of DI water) to produce precipitates. After the addition of NaOH aqueous solution was completed, the mixture was stirred at 90 ℃ for another 1.5 h. The mixture was cooled to RT and filtered, washed with DI water and ethanol. Finally, the product was collected after dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h in an oven.

FeyPz-10 was prepared by using the same experiments process but with the pCD-x solution (0.23 g of pCD-6/10 mL of DI water) instead of the DI water. In addition, a series of FeyPz was prepared by altering the amounts of reactants, such as the amount of pCD, Fe3+/Fe2+ mole ratio, and the amount of NaOH, which were marked as factor-A, B and C, respectively (as shown in Table S1
-S4). The procedure of the two-step preparation of FeyPz is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The starting uranium stock solution was prepared by dissolving UO2(NO3)2٠6H2O in 100 mL of DI water and then two drops of concentrated HNO3 (ρ = 1.42 g mL−1) was added. Next, the solution was transferred to a 250 mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with DI water. Finally, a 4.2 mmol L-1 (1000 mg L-1) of U(VI) solution was prepared. A low concentration of U(VI) solution with a desired pH value was obtained by diluting the uranium stock solution and the pH levels of the solution was adjusted by 0.01 mol L-1 HNO3 or NaOH aqueous solution.

Briefly, 10 mg of adsorbent was placed in 10 mL of a U(VI) solution. After the mixture was shaken for 2 h at a speed of 300 rpm it was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter and the filtrate was analyzed to quantify the remaining U(VI) concentration with the arsenazo III spectrophotometric method [9,14,20–21]. The adsorption capacity of U(VI) onto the adsorbent (q e, mmol g−1) was calculated using equation (1).
(1)
q e
=
(
C 0
-
C e
) V
m

Here, C 0 and C e (mmol L-1) are the U(VI) concentration in the solution at the initial and at final equilibrium, respectively, V (L) is the volume of the solution, and m (g) is the mass of the adsorbent. All experiments were carried out three times, and the standard deviations were also calculated.

For recyclability test, U(VI)-loaded adsorbent was stirred in a 10 mL of 0.5 mol L-1 Na2CO3 overnight. The solid was collected by filtration, washed with DI water. After being dried in a vacuum, the adsorption experiments were performed with a same U(VI) solution. The reusability of the adsorbent was repeated five times.

The column adsorption tests were following the same procedure reported in our previous study [20]. An organic glass column with its diameter of 10 mm was used. The breakthrough curve was obtained by plotting the C't/C'0 versus total volume of effluent (V eff, mL). The total U(VI) adsorption capacity (q total, mg g−1) was calculated by the following equation:

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analysis of phosphorus content by the titrimetric method
ce:section.ce:para: The ammonium molybdate solution was prepared as follows: 20 g of ammonium molybdate [(NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O] was dissolved in a solution of 150 mL of hot water (50 ℃) and 40 mL of NH3·H2O (ρ = 0.90 g mL−1). After the solution was vigorously stirred for 5 min and cooled to RT, it was poured slowly into a solution of 150 mL of DI water (50 ℃) and 150 mL of HNO3 (ρ = 1.42 g mL−1).

The phosphorus (P element) contents of pCD-6 and FeyPz-10 were measured by the acid-base titrimetric method [22]. Firstly, acid digestion was performed on a certain amount of sample (m 0, 60 mg) in a conical flask using 10 mL of 1:1 HCl (ρ = 1.19 g mL−1)/HNO3 (ρ = 1.42 g mL−1) and heated on a heating plate for 1 h (100 ℃). After cooling down to RT, 5 mL of HCl (ρ = 1.19 g mL−1) and 60 mL of hot water (50 ℃) were added and at last to obtain a clear and transparent solution. Secondly, the production of ammonium phosphomolybdate precipitation were conducted as follows: a suitable amount of NH3·H2O (ρ = 0.90 g mL−1) was added into the previous solution until a little Fe(OH)3 sediment was generated and even not disappear. And then, a suitable amount of HNO3 (ρ = 1.42 g mL−1) was added until the sediment was just disappearing. Next, 5 mL of HNO3 (ρ = 1.42 g mL−1), 10 mL of ammonium nitrate solution (300 g L-1) and 100 mL of the ammonium molybdate solution were added in turn. Subsequently, the mixture was immersed in a water batch (50 ℃) for 15 min. After vigorous stirring for 3 min and let it stand for about 2 h at RT before filtering it through microporous filter membrane under reduced pressure. The filter cake was firstly washed with diluted HNO3 (0.3 mol L-1) four times and with DI water until the filtrate was neutral. Finally, put the filter cake and filter membrane in a conical flask and 50 mL DI water was added immediately. Then, pipetted 15 mL (V 1) of standard NaOH solution (C 1, 1.0 mol L-1) into the conical flask under stirred conditions and the precipitation was completely dissolved. Being stirred for 5 min, 3 drops of phenolphthalein ethanol solution (50 g L-1) was added. At this point, the mixture was red-color. To determine the end-point of titration by using 1.0 mol L-1 standard HNO3 solution (C 2) as a titrant to titrate the mixture solution from red color to colorless. The weight ratio of P element (w p, %) in samples was calculated by the following equation:
(3)
w p
=
(
C 1
×
V 1
-
C 2
×
V 2
) × 1.291
m 0
× 1000
× 100 %

C 1 and C 2 (mol L-1) are the concentrations of standard NaOH solution and standard HNO3 solution, respectively, V 1 and V 2 (mL) are the volume of pipetted standard NaOH solution and consumed standard HNO3 solution, respectively, m 0 (g) is the mass of sample, 1.291 (g mol−1) was the molecular weight of 1/24P.

The carbon (C) and hydrogen (H) contents of the samples were measured by combustion analysis using a Vario EL cube elemental analyzer through employing the CHNS method (Elementar, Germany). The iron (Fe) content was determined using atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS, ContrAA 300, Analytikjena, Germany). The phosphorus content was measured by the titrimetric method. The surface morphology and element composition of samples were examined by a Phenom Pure desktop scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Phenom, Netherlands) containing a Phenom-World B.V. energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy analysis system. The morphology and size of pCD-6 was observed using the atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Dimension Icon, Bruker, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected using a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN instrument (USA) operated at an accelerating voltage of 300 kV. The TEM elemental distribution mapping analysis was carried out using a Oxford MAX-80 energy dispersive X-ray (EDS) detector. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired by a INVENIO spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with a Platinum ATR (Attenuated Total Reflection) accessory on samples without use of KBr in the 4000–400 cm−1 wavenumber range. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted to identify the crystallographic structure of the product and to observe any changes in the final sample structure after alter the synthesis conditions using a PANalytical X'Pert Pro powder X-ray diffractometer (Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.540598 Å, Netherlands) in 0.033° scan step size within the 2θ range from 3 to 80°. Magnetic hysteresis measurements were performed on a SQUID vibrating sample magnetometer (MPMS-3, Quantum Design, USA). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area and the pore size characterization were measured using a nitrogen adsorption–desorption curves at 77.35 K on an Autosorb-iQ automated analyzer (Quantachrome, USA). The BET specific surface area was determined according to the nitrogen adsorption data in the relative pressure P/P0 range from 0.05 to 0.30. Total pore volume was determined from the amount of gas adsorbed at the P/P0 of 0.994. Pore size distribution was derived from the desorption branches via the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out on a NETZSCH STA449F5 thermogravimetric analyzer (Netzsch, Germany) in the temperature range of RT to 800 ℃ using a heating rate of 10 ℃ s−1 under an air atmosphere. The point of zero charge (pHPZC) was estimated by the batch equilibrium method [23]. The pHPZC of the FeyPz-10 was measured by adding 20 mg of the adsorbent to several 50 mL centrifuge tubes containing 10 mL 0.01 mol L-1 KNO3 solution and the initial pH value (pHi) of the solutions were adjusted by using the 0.1 mol L-1 HNO3 or KOH aqueous solutions in the pH range of 2.0–10.0. After shaken for 24 h at RT, the equilibrium pH values (pHf) were recorded. The analysis of grain size distribution was performed for Fe3O4 and FeyPz-10 in aqueous solution by a LS 13 320 laser diffraction (LD) particle size analyzer (Beckman Coulter Inc., USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to study the surface characteristic and the composition of functional groups in samples, which was conducted by a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) equipped with a monochromatic Al-Kα X-ray source operated at 1486.6 eV. All binding energies were referenced to the XPS C1s peak from adventitious carbon at 284.8 eV. The concentration of U(VI) in solution was detected by measuring the absorbance at wavelength of 652 nm on a Lambda 650 double-beam UV–visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA).
Fig. 1 shows the detailed preparation procedure of Fe3O4 and FeyPz, which was synthesized in the absence and presence of pCD, respectively.

The pCD was produced can be taken for through the condensation polymerization reaction and the carbonization under experimental hydrothermal conditions [24]. FeyPz was synthesized by a two-step method, including the hydrothermal method (step I) and then the co-precipitation approach (step II), wherein a precursor solution of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in the presences of pCD solution was first prepared. The co-precipitation occurs follows the addition of base in the precursor solution under a desired temperature. In order to synthesize FeyPz with high adsorption property, we have optimized the synthesis condition by changing the amounts of EA and H3PO4 for synthesis of pCD, the amount of pCD, Fe3+/Fe2+ mole ratio, and the amount of NaOH. Change of the functional groups and crystal structure in FeyPz were examined using the FT-IR and XRD techniques as shown in Fig. 2-3
. By detailed IR and XRD characterization of a series of product (the reaction conditions for each samples are shown in Table S1
-S4), it was found that the addition of the amount of P and the amount of NaOH are very important for the formation of FeyPz-10.

In detail, if the reactant without of P, only Fe3O4 was observed. And with excess addition of the amount of NaOH, the crystal phase of the product would convert from amorphous iron (II/III) phosphate to Fe3O4, at the meantime, with a spot of NaOH, the production yield was low and some other ferric compound might be generated.

Also, different synthetic methods were used to prepare FeyPz. It was found that the well FePO4٠2H2O crystals were produced by using the one-pot approach (150 ℃, 5 h, FeyPz-15). And the prepared FeyPz-17 were mixture crystal of FePO4٠2H2O and Fe3O4 which were originating from only physical mixing of the pCD-6 with Fe3O4 (90 ℃, 1.5 h). Furthermore, H3PO4 instead of pCD-6 was also chosen for the preparation of FeyPz-18. Similar results of FT-IR and XRD were observed with FeyPz-10.

With respect to the U(VI) adsorption efficiency, our results indicated that FeyPz-10 exhibited best metal ion adsorption performance, suggesting that with proper control of the experimental conditions, FeyPz-10 can be successfully synthesized. More importantly, when got additional pCD-6 in the reactants which used for the preparation of Fe3O4 was another result.

The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2) have been conducted to figure out the difference among Fe3O4, FePO4٠2H2O (FeyPz-15) and FeyPz-10. In the FT-IR spectrum of Fe3O4, a strong absorption band is observed with maxima at 552 cm−1, which can be assigned to Fe-O bond. Additionally, two band at 3233 and 1642 cm−1 can be assigned to O–H stretching vibration and symmetrical bending vibration, respectively, which derives from the hydration of water molecular and hydroxyl groups [25]. In the case of FePO4٠2H2O (FeyPz-15) obtained by the one-pot approach, two new intense band appeared at 987 and 544 cm−1, which can be owing to the P-OH stretching vibration and Fe-O stretching vibration, respectively. All bands observed are in agreement with previous assignments for in the literature [5,26].

However, compared with FeyPz-15, the peak of P-OH band was shifted from 987 cm−1 to a high frequency of 1005 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of FeyPz-10. and the peak appeared at 567 cm−1 which testifies the presence of Fe-O bond [8,27]. Otherwise, the P-OH band became intense when more pCD-1 or more P or less NaOH was added. Therefore, on the basis the analysis data mentioned above, the formation of Fe3O4 is found when little pCD-1 or 0 mL of P or higher quantities of NaOH adding in the reactant. The FeyPz-10 and Fe3O4 exhibited utterly different peaks, showing that the addition of P induces the formation of iron (II/III) phosphates.

The XRD patterns of various FeyPz samples with the change of the synthesis condition parameters are illustrated in Fig. 3. It is found that the Fe3O4 crystal was appeared when adding less pCD (0.115 g of pCD-1) or under the absence of P adding (0 mL of P) or more NaOH was added. The diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 were found at 30.1°, 35.5°, 43.4°, 53.5°, 57.1° and 62.8° which can be assigned to the and planes (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440), respectively. The results can be easily indexed to the phase of magnetite Fe3O4 (JCPDS 01–1111 or 03–0863) [28]. Specifically, the FePO4٠2H2O crystal (FeyPz-15) was produced and proved when all the reactants were added together, those diffraction peaks appear at 13.6°, 18.2°, 19.0°, 20.4°, 21.7°, 24.7°, 26.8°, 28.0°, and 32.2° that can be indexed to (011), (020), (110), (002), (−111), (120), (−121), (−112) and (−122) crystal planes of FePO4٠2H2O (JCPDS 15–0390). Interesting, adding pCD-6 into the as-prepared Fe3O4 would lead to a mixed phase (physical mixing, FeyPz-17). The XRD pattern of FeyPz-17 can be regarded as a combination of the FePO4٠2H2O and Fe3O4 crystals. In addition, apart from a broad diffraction peak, no other diffraction peaks of species are detected in the XRD pattern of FeyPz-10, demonstrating that the composition of FeyPz-10 was iron (II/III) phosphates rather than Fe3O4 and FePO4٠2H2O and the amorphous nature of FeyPz-10. Furthermore, the crystal size of samples was also calculated using the Scherrer equation. The data was listed hereafter: FeyPz-9 = 12.8 nm, FeyPz-13 = 11.0 nm, FeyPz-14 = 14.7 nm, FeyPz-15 (FePO4٠2H2O) = 65.9 nm, FeyPz-16 (Fe3O4) = 17.3 nm, FeyPz-17 = 34.3 nm, and the other samples = impossible to be determined. Also, the result for Fe3O4 is in agreement with the result of TEM analysis as shown later. Consequently, according to the results of FT-IR and XRD analysis, although the synthesis conditions of Fe3O4 and FeyPz-10 are the same, thus it seems that the presence of pCD-6 in the reaction medium would preferentially react with both ferric and ferrous for the formation of the iron (II/III) phosphate, which can be explained according to the following reactions (K sp = the solubility product constant) [29]:
xFe3+ + Fe2+ + yPO4 3- + zOH- ⇌Fex 3+Fe2+(PO4)y(OH)z (8)

Fig. S3 shows the AFM image of the pCD-6. It can be observed that the synthesized pCD-6 have uniform size with a particle size of 33.95 ± 6.13 nm and with a thickness of 1.46 ± 0.49 nm (n = 50). The SEM-EDX analysis ( Fig. S4 ) indicate that the pCD-6 possess C, N, O and P element. Moreover, the weight percentage of P element in pCD-6 was estimated to be about 20.60 wt% (weight percentage = wt%), which value is close to the analysis result of the titrimetric method (18.14 ± 0.18 wt%), implying that the P element has been doped into the pCD-6 in success.

The surface morphology, element composition and distribution of FeyPz-10 were examined by SEM. As shown in Fig. 4
a, the as-prepared FeyPz-10 featured the uniform nanoparticle with the size less than 40 nm. The elemental distribution mapping images in Fig. 4 b confirmed the successful incorporation of P element and the uniform distribution of O, Fe and P in FeyPz-10 nanomaterial. Besides, SEM-EDX analysis gave a value of 2.2 for the Fe/P atomic ratio in FeyPz-10 sample (Fig. 4 c), suggesting that the final formula for FeyPz-10 concludes to Fe 3x: P 1.5x. Similar results were also observed by the XPS (Fe/P = 2.0), AAS (Fe, 33.27 ± 1.80 wt%) and titration (P, 8.60 ± 0.01 wt%) analysis (Fe/P = 2.1) (Table 1 ).

The morphology of Fe3O4 and FeyPz-10 was demonstrated by the TEM images (Fig. 5 and S6). It can be observed that the Fe3O4 and FeyPz-10 have different morphology features. The synthesized Fe3O4 have a square shape with its size in the range of 5–15 nm. In the case of FeyPz-10 (Fig. 5
b), it was found that the addition of p-CD into the raw material of synthesized Fe3O4 changed the product which exhibited a nearly spherical-shaped particles with an average particle size of 28.2 ± 5.6 nm, suggesting the pCD-6 doping in the lattice structure of Fe3O4.

Furthermore, the elemental distribution mapping of the FeyPz-10 indicates homogeneous distribution of the O, P, and Fe elements throughout the sample (Fig. 5 c). The distribution of carbon element is too weak to be found, which could be ascribed to a small quantity of carbon in product. This result is very similar to that obtained with the SEM images and elemental analysis (EA) data which showed that the weight percentages of C, H, N and S of FeyPz-10 were 1.59 ± 0.24, 2.62 ± 0.16, 0.45 ± 0.09 and 0.42 ± 0.19 wt%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific surface area and pore structure and magnetic properties study
ce:section.ce:para: In order to acquire the porous structure of Fe3O4 and FeyPz-10, BET specific surface area and pore size distribution were measured. As indicated Fig. 6 , both samples possess a IV type N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms with an H1 hysteresis loop and also show a large number of mesopores in pore size distribution, implying the mesoporous feature of the materials [32]. The BET specific surface area of Fe3O4 and FeyPz-10 were measured to be 98.8 and 229.6 m2/g, respectively, suggesting FeyPz-10 has a large surface area which is favorable for the improvement of adsorption activities. Also, the total pore volume of Fe3O4 and FeyPz-10 were found to be 0.361 and 0.958 cm3/g, respectively. The result for Fe3O4 is similar to the previously reported values [33]. The pore size of Fe3O4 was mainly distributed in 6–18 nm and FeyPz-10 showed a narrow pore size distribution at 3–5 nm. It can be speculated that the Fe3O4 was composed of different aggregate particles and the introducing of P could promote the formation of amorphous FeyPz-10 which state facilitates to obtain small pore structure that consistent with the previous literature for Fe-doped CePO4 [32].

The surface chemical states of elemental compositions of Fe3O4 and FeyPz-10 were further investigated by the XPS analysis. The XPS survey spectra of the Fe3O4 shows two peaks located at 710.73 and 529.96 eV which corresponds to Fe 2p and O 1 s, respectively. For FeyPz-10, in addition to the previously mentioned peaks, two clear peaks are observed at 191.10 and 133.41 eV which can be assigned to the P 2 s and P 2p, respectively (Fig. 8
a). The results further demonstrate that the P doping was in success.

To obtain some quantitative information, the high-resolution spectra XPS of Fe 2p3/2 peaks of the Fe3O4 and FeyPz-10 were divided into three peaks. As shown in Fig. 8 b. The three peaks were related to Feth 3+ (tetrahedral), Feoh 3+ (octahedral) and Feoh 2+ (octahedral), which located at 712.13, 710.84 and 709.86 eV, respectively [34–35]. Based on the relative area of the peaks, the ratio of Feth 3+ : Feoh 3+ : Feoh 2+ is 0.91:1.04:1.00 for Fe3O4 and the atomic ratio of Fe3+ : Fe2+ is 1.95 which was matched well with the theoretical stoichiometric of magnetite Fe3O4 that can be alternatively expressed as Fe2O3٠FeO. The almost same peaks are also observed for the FeyPz-10 but the relative intensity of each peak is different. For FeyPz-10, the ratio of Feth 3+ : Feoh 3+ : Feoh 2+ is calculated about 0.95:1.18:1.00 and the atomic ratio of Fe3+ : Fe2+ is 2.14. Based on the atomic ratio of Fe/P was equal to 2.0, the composition of FeyPz-10 could be written as Fe2 3+Fe2+(PO4)1.5(OH)3.5.

Moreover, the O1s high-resolution XPS spectrum of the Fe3O4 (Fig. 8 c) shows two different contributions located at 531.35 and 529.95 eV, which are assigned to Fe-OH and Fe-O-Fe, respectively. Meanwhile, for FeyPz-10, besides the two peaks, a new peak is observed at 530.81 eV, which is associated with P = O/Fe-O-P, indicating the presence of phosphoric group. Peaks position were reported for Fe-OH/P-OH (531.6–532.5 eV), P = O/Fe-O-P (530.5–531.3 eV) and Fe-O-Fe (530.0–530.5 eV) [36].

The high-resolution P 2p XPS spectrum of FeyPz-10 can be divided into two peaks (Fig. 8 d), the two intense peak that located at 134.04 and 133.06 eV can be respectively attributed to P = O and P-O/P-O-Fe, respectively. There are no peaks for Fe3O4 observed in the P 2p XPS spectra of Fe3O4. Peaks position were reported for P = O (135.24 eV), P-O (134.27 eV) and P-O-Fe (133.5–134.0 eV) [36–37].
The XPS analysis has demonstrated that the Fe3O4 have successfully been synthesized and the accurate composition of FeyPz-10 was Fe3(PO4)1.5(OH)3.5.

TGA has been used to determine the thermal stability of the Fe3O4 and FeyPz-10. The TGA curves of Fe3O4 and FeyPz-10 showed a different thermal behavior. As shown in Fig. 9
a, the weight loss was 15.29 wt% for Fe3O4 in the temperature range from 30 to 450 ℃. This weight loss can be accounted for by the removal of adsorbed water in the sample. For FeyPz-10, one can see that two weight losses and one weight increase was found. The first weight loss (about 11.41 wt%) below 200 ℃ can be attributed to physically adsorbed water. And the second weight loss (3.16 wt%) between 200 and 350 ℃ can be due to the removal of OH in FeyPz-10. From 350 to 580 ℃, a weak weight increase region was observed which can be owing to the oxidation reaction of Fe2+ to Fe3+ in air. The residue weight of FeyPz-10 was 84.44 wt%. This indicates that the FeyPz-10 possess a good thermal stability.Fig. 10.

Fig. 9 b shows the pHf versus pHi plots for FeyPz-10 with corresponding pHPZC value of 7.72. For the pH value of solution lower than pHPZC, the adsorbent was positively charged. Conversely, it was negatively charged at pH higher than pHPZC. This pHPZC value is similar to that of synthetic hydroxyapatite (4.1–8.6) [3,10,23].

Fig. S7 shows the particle size distribution of Fe3O4 and FeyPz-10 in aqueous solution. It can be observed that the mean size of particles of FeyPz-16 (Fe3O4) and FeyPz-10 were 109 and 96 nm, respectively. This may be owing to the occurrence of particles agglomeration [3].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Efficient adsorption of U(VI) by FeyPz-10
ce:section.ce:para:
Effective adsorption of U(VI) can be achieved by tailoring the optimum the amount of raw materials to add within the synthetic reaction of FeyPz ( Table S1
-S4, Fig. 10 and S8). The results show that the adsorption performance of FeyPz is significantly influenced by the synthesis conditions and adsorption capacity governed by many variables, including quantity of P, the amount of pCD added, molar ratio of Fe3+/Fe2+, and amount of NaOH. Also, it was shown that the changes of the addition amount of P in step I process did cause significant change to the U(VI) adsorption capacity. By comparison, the FeyPz-10 (0.67 mmol g−1) showed four times higher adsorption capacity to U(VI) against that of FeyPz-9 (0.17 mmol g−1) in which none P was introduced. Notably, the adsorption capacity of FeyPz-10 (0.659 mmol g−1) is 36.6 times higher than that of FeyPz-15 (FePO4٠2H2O, 0.019 mmol g−1) and is 684.5% higher than that of FeyPz-16 (Fe3O4, 0.084 mmol g−1). Considering that FeyPz-10 and FeyPz-15 have similar chemical composition, the higher adsorption capacity for FeyPz-10 should be attributed to the presence of Fe2+ and their different structure. As noted above, the results can suggest U(VI) reduction to U(IV) during the adsorption process [6].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the adsorption of U(VI)
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 11
a shows the adsorption capacity of U(VI) onto FeyPz-10 within a range of pH value 2.0–4.5. The adsorption test results shows that the adsorption capacity of U(VI) increased by increasing the pH value from 2.0 to 4.5. These results can be owing to the positively charged adsorbent surface is not favor of the adsorption of UO2 2+ cations and H+ would compete with UO2 2+ to a same adsorption sits in a lower pH value medium, otherwise, the H+ in the U(VI) solution and the degree of positive charge on the adsorbent became less at a higher pH value which promotes the interplay between U(VI) and adsorbents and thus the adsorption capacity was increased [5]. Also, the mainly U(VI) species was UO2 2+ at a pH value lower than 4.5 [26,38] and it was observed the occurrence of the hydrolysis and precipitation of U(VI) at a higher pH value [14]. Therefore, the pH value of 4.5 was chosen for the following adsorption studies.

As seen from Fig. 11 b, the equilibrium adsorption capacity of FeyPz-10 was reached equilibrium within 120 min with its value of 0.45 mmol g−1. The exhibited fast uranium adsorption speed compared with the utilization of some other adsorbents for U(VI) adsorption, that typically taken several hours (mixed metal oxides, hafnium phosphonate) to reach equilibrium [7,13]. Moreover, the U(VI) uptake data were then fitted with a pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models [1,39].

The pseudo-first-order kinetic model can be expressed as follows:

where k 1 (min−1) and q t (mmol g−1) are the pseudo-first-order rate constant of adsorption and the adsorption capacity at a time (t), respectively.

The pseudo-second-order kinetic model can be expressed as follows:

where k 2 (g mmol−1 min−1) is the pseudo-second-order rate constant of adsorption.

The analysis of the adsorption kinetics revealed that the adsorption process of the FeyPz-10 fitted well with the pseudo-second-order dynamic model (R 2 value higher than 0.99), indicating that the U(VI) adsorption by FeyPz-10 was mainly chemisorption-controlled process [39].

The influence of ionic strength on U(VI) adsorption performance was studied by choosing 0–1.0 mol L-1 NaNO3 (Fig. 11 c). It can be observed a slight reduction of the U(VI) adsorption capacities as the concentration of NaNO3 rises from 0 to 0.01 mol L-1, which implied that the inner-sphere surface complexation was main adsorption mechanism that could be attributed to the formation of ≡Fe-OH2 +-UO2(OH)3 - [40–41].With further increase of NaNO3 concentration from 0.01 to 1.0 mol L-1, the concentration of NaNO3 have no effect on the U(VI) extraction, indicating that the adsorption performance of FeyPz-10 was stable under high ionic strength, which suggested that the adsorption of U(VI) using FeyPz-10 was dominated by a chemical reduction mechanism.

Effect of initial U(VI) concentration on the adsorption capacities of FeyPz-10 was shown in Fig. S9 . It is obvious that the adsorption capacity of U(VI) increased with the increasing initial U(VI) concentration, and then reached saturation. This may be caused by the more metal ion at a higher concentration. And the free active sits was limited when further increasing the concentration. Furtherly, the experimental data was fitted using the Langmuir [1], the Freundich [14,39], and the Dubinin-Radushkevich (D-R) isotherm models [42].

The Langmuir isotherm model can be depicted as follows:
(11)

where b (L mmol−1) and q m (mmol g−1) are the Langmuir constant and the theoretical maximum adsorption capacity, respectively.

where β (mol2 kJ−2) and q D-R (mol g−1) are the D-R constant and the D-R theoretical adsorption capacity, respectively. ε is the Polanyi potential. R (0.008314 kJ mol−1 K−1) and T (298 K) are gas constant and the absolute temperature at which the adsorption was conducted, respectively. E (kJ mol−1) is the mean adsorption energy.

As seen from Fig. 11 d, the non-linear regressions were used in fitting the Langmuir and the Freundlich models. The adsorption isotherm is in good agreement with the Langmuir isotherm model with the correction coefficients higher than 0.99, suggesting that the adsorption of U(VI) on FeyPz-10 was caused by a mono-layer homogeneous adsorption manner. The theoretical maximum adsorption capacity of FeyPz-10 was 1.22 mmol U g−1, which is comparable with different types of uranium adsorbents ( Fig. S10 ). Meantime, this adsorption capacity is less than that of zero-valent iron-polyaniline-graphene aerogel (q m = 1.47 mmol g−1) [38], but our material is advantageous in that it can be prepared via a simple chemical reaction and reaction equipment.

The Gibbs free energy (ΔG°, kJ mol−1) of U(VI) adsorption onto FeyPz-10 was calculated from the Langmuir constant (b), following the equation below [43]:
(14)
Δ
G °
= - R T ln

( 55.5 × 1000 × b )

where ΔG° is the standard Gibbs free energy change of the adsorption processes, K c is the standard equilibrium constant, 55.5 (mol L-1) is the activity of pure water in solution which vale is equal to its moles concentration. The ΔG° of U(VI) adsorption by FeyPz-10 was calculated to be −40.8 kJ mol−1, implying a thermodynamically favorable adsorption process [43].
The value of E for FeyPz-10 is 16.7 kJ mol−1, indicating that the adsorption type of U(VI) onto FeyPz-10 is chemical adsorption [9–10,42].

In addition, the recyclability of the adsorbent was determined in order to evaluate the economic cost of the adsorbent. The result ( Fig. S11 and Table S5 ) showed that the adsorbent can be regenerated by aqueous carbonate treatment (0.5 mol L-1 solution of Na2CO3) without losing its adsorption capacity. The adsorbent maintained a high U(VI) adsorption capacity even after being reused five times (q e = 0.79 ± 0.09 mmol g−1 at the 5th adsorption test).

To evaluate the dynamic adsorption properties of the adsorbent, the fixed-bed column adsorption test was studied. Fig. 12 shows the U(VI) removal breakthrough curve for FeyPz-10. A column adsorption capacity of 196.0 mg g−1 for FeyPz-10 was obtained, which is higher than the q total of phosphorylated carbon aerogel (64.5 mg g−1, pH = 5.5) for reported previously [5,44]. Further, the data was fitted by the Thomas model [5].

The linearized form of Thomas equation is as follows:

where K T (mL min−1 mg−1) and q T (mg g−1) are the Thomas rate constant and the maximum adsorption capacity of the column, respectively, υ (1.09 mL min−1) is the flow rate of the U(VI) solution. ttotal (min) is the total time of effluent.

As shown in Fig. 12, the R 2 of Thomas model is 0.980, indicating the model could reflect U(VI) adsorption on FeyPz-10 reasonably and the external and internal diffusion were not the limiting steps [44]. The value of K T and q T are calculated to be 0.05 mL min−1 mg−1 and 207.8 mg g−1, respectively. The theoretical value was close to the experimental q total (196.0 mg g−1). Generally, the FeyPz-10 display a promising potential and efficient for the removal of U(VI) from acidic aqueous solution.

Although phosphoric derivative possesses strong coordinating ability for UO2 2+, but two different results were obtained for FePO4٠2H2O and FeyPz-10 that the former has lower adsorption capacity for U(VI), suggesting that complexing interaction and ion-exchange are not the main mechanism for the high U(VI) adsorption capacity onto FeyPz-10.

To uncover the U(VI) adsorption mechanism, we firstly measured the pH values of the U(VI) solution before and after the adsorption. As depicted in Fig. S12 , the equilibrium pH value after U(VI) adsorption was higher than the initial pH, illustrating a decrease in H+ ions in solution after U(VI) adsorption by FeyPz-10. This result might be regard as the existence of the protonation of the –OH functional groups on surface of the FeyPz-10. The reaction can be expressed as follows:
≡Fe-OH + H+ ⇌≡Fe-OH2 + (17)

A similar result was reported for hydroxyapatite/biochar materials with increase in the final solution pH after Cu(II) adsorption [45]. A reduction reaction can also explain the increase in pH value after U(VI) adsorption, as the consumption of proton for the reduction. The reactions can be written as below:
Next, the interaction between FeyPz-10 and U(VI) was investigated by using a variety of spectroscopic technologies, including SEM-EDX, FT-IR and XPS.

After adsorption of U(VI), the EDX analysis was used to study the composition of the product, and the elemental distribution mapping images (Fig. 13 ) shows that apart from P, O and Fe elements, U element is also uniformly distributed in FeyPz-10-U, confirming the successful adsorption of U(VI) onto the adsorbent. The calculated Fe/P atomic ratio of the FeyPz-10-U is identical with that of FeyPz-10, confirming the FeyPz-10 adsorbents are stable in a acidic aqueous solution and ion-exchange is not the main mechanism for U(VI) onto FeyPz-10. It is worth to note that the ion-exchange is the main mechanism for metal ions adsorption onto previously reported zirconium phosphate [46].

Comparing the FT-IR spectrum of FeyPz-10 before and after treatment with U(VI), in spite of the two spectra shows most the same signals, but a new peak is appeared at 904 cm−1 which can be ascribe to the antisymmetric stretching vibration of UO2 2+ (Fig. 14
a) [19,26], that exhibits a different position with UO2(NO3)6٠H2O (960 cm−1) [1], implying strong interactions exists between U(VI) and FeyPz-10, which revealed similarities with spectrum of the previously literature for Fe(II)-Al(III) layered-double-hydroxides [6].

Furthermore, FeyPz-10 before and after adsorbing U(VI) exhibited identical patterns (Fig. 14 b), reflecting that the adsorption of U(VI) do not cause damage to the initial structure of FeyPz-10, which implied that FeyPz-10 possess good stability.

Fig. S13 shows the XPS of FeyPz-10 after treatment with U(VI), which obviously exhibits a distinctive peak at 382.29 eV attributable to the element U 4f-bonding energy, showing that uranium was successfully loaded onto the FeyPz-10. In depth XPS analysis of FeyPz-10 and FeyPz-10-U(VI) was carried out and the results is shown in Fig. 15 and Table S6 . Generally, changes of intensity of peak at a given chemical state are connected with the number of atoms in that state.

Deconvolution of the Fe 2p3/2 spectrum for FeyPz-10-U(VI) (Fig. 15 a) revealed similarities with spectrum of the FeyPz-10. Three peaks of iron element were still found, representing the peak of Fe3+ (tetrahedral) (712.85 eV), the peak of Fe3+ (octahedral) (711.42 eV), and the peak of Fe2+ (octahedral) (710.35 eV). The remarkable decrease of the intensity of the Fe2+ (octahedral) peak for FeyPz-10-U(VI) sample (23.33 at%) as compared to that of FeyPz-10 (31.89 at%) and a increase in intensity of the Fe3+ (tetrahedral) peak from 30.38 at% for FeyPz-10 to 39.81 at% for FeyPz-10-U(VI) suggests that the oxidation of Fe2+ occurs in interacting with U(VI) during the U(VI) adsorption process [6].

The detail high-resolution XPS analysis of the oxygen element in FeyPz-10 after U(VI) adsorption showed that three peaks were observed (Fig. 15 b), including the Fe-OH/P-OH (532.08 eV), the P = O/Fe-O-P (531.17 eV), and the Fe-O-Fe/U = O/uranium oxide (530.17 eV) [39,47–48]. For FeyPz-10-U(VI), an distinct positive shift from 531.49 to 532.08 eV at the binding energy of the Fe-OH/P-OH peak, along with the decreased intensity of this peak, showing this site participates in U(VI) capture which might be due to the uranyl ions partially complexation with oxygen atom in some of the OH group from the Fe-OH/P-OH groups [49].

Comparing the P 2p peaks of FeyPz-10 before and after treatment of U(VI) (Fig. 15 c), the two binding energy peaks located at 134.01 and 133.21 eV are assigned to P = O and P-O/P-O-Fe, respectively, retains unchanged, but the intensity of the P-O/P-O-Fe was decreased from 46.79 to 46.11 at%, showing that there is indeed no interaction between P = O from phosphate group and U(VI) and the interaction between uranium atom and the oxygen atoms in P-O/P-O-Fe occurred.

The chemical states of uranium element in the FeyPz-10-U sample were further studied by high-resolution XPS (Fig. 15 d). The U 4f spectrum exhibits peaks at 392.96 and 382.27 eV which is widely recognized as characteristic of U(IV) species (U 4f5/2 and U 4f7/2, respectively), in addition to features at 393.74 and 383.77 eV that can be assigned to U(VI) species (U 4f5/2 and U 4f7/2, respectively) [26,50]. This results are indicative of the presence of both U(VI) and U(IV), implying the role of a reduction-based capture mechanism, based on the reduction of U(VI) to U(IV) by Fe(II) exist in the adsorbent, similar to the previously reported Fe(II)-Al(III) layered-double-hydroxides [6] and Fe nanocrystals embedded into N-doped carbon nanospheres [26], both supported by the existence of Fe(II).

According to the XPS analysis result, it is rational to conclude that the U(VI) adsorption onto FeyPz-10 is main a adsorption-reduction process [26], and the OH from Fe-OH/P-OH groups in FeyPz-10 take part in coordination reaction (Fig. 16 ).

In summary, we have successfully developed a new kind of iron phosphate material, FeyPz. The synthesis procedure of FeyPz was categorized into two steps: pCD synthesis (step I) and the co-precipitation (step II). The functional groups, crystal structure, and uranium adsorption capacity of FeyPz by changing the reaction factors were systematically investigated and compared. The amount of P or pCD, the mole ratio of Fe3+/Fe2+ and the amount of NaOH has a remarkable impact on the adsorption performance and structure of FeyPz. As a result, FeyPz-10 (0.659 mmol g−1) exhibit best adsorption performance than that of FePO4·2H2O (FeyPz-15, 0.019 mmol g−1) and Fe3O4 (FeyPz-16, 0.084 mmol g−1). The maximum uranium adsorption capacity of 1.22 mmol g−1 from aqueous solutions for FeyPz-10 was obtained. Moreover, the saturated U(VI) adsorption capacity of the FeyPz-10 reaches 0.87 mmol g−1, which is higher than that of phosphorylated carbon aerogel. Additionally, a slight adsorption capacity decay is observed after 5th cycle of adsorption–desorption. Furthermore, XPS analysis indicated that the presence of Fe2+ in FeyPz-10 contributed significantly to the U(VI) adsorption performance. Owing to the simplicity and scalable synthetic approach for production of iron (II/III) phosphate material, it would represent an important potential that will boost the application of inorganic adsorbent for uranium-containing waste-water management.
